1uler, horace ". MCKINNEY, Ian. W..
Tarpley, Frank H. Wolgamot and


Emma l.. Watson, have also been con-
victed of conspiracy to defraud the
Government of it's public lands and


L. S. District Attorney John II. Hall
has been summarily removed from


Office and for the good of the service
But these are not the only ones
involved. In the back ground are
the big lumber kings of Wisconsin.
Michigan, Minnesota and other
States in the Middle West, who
have played the role Of fence" in
the systematic robberies of the Gov
eminent. They have supplied the
| money for proofs of pretended
homestead entries, for bribing no.
taries, County officials, U. s. Com
missioners, and for corrupting spe-
cial agents of the land department
who were sent out to investigate


the claims. Large sums Of hush
money have been advanced since the
Government investigations began, ind


all of this is supposed to have come
from the lumber men who were the


beneficaries of the frauds.


All of these lumber men dealt
through agents. A Portland man


was their principal agent and during


the last four or five years he is said


to have purchased for hi sprincipals


100000 acres of Government land in
Washington and oregon.


1t is generally expected that this
man will be indicted. His indict-
ment will be apt to lead to disclos-


ures involving the wealthy lumber


men for whom he acted.


1t is said that at least rs per cent
of the homestead entries made in
Oregon within the past ten years
have been fraudulent. Within the
last II years numerous forest re-
serves have been created by the
Government in the Western States.
Under the Scrip law any settler who


has taken up homestead claim upon
| lands which were subsequently in
cluded in forest reserve has the OPP
ton Of either perfecting his title
and retaining his homestead, or of
surrendering the homestead and TAX
| ing in lieu thereof, scrip which en-
titles him to an equal amount of
Government land anywhere else.
| Advance information as to the
boundaries of proposed forest re-


serves was constantly sold to specu-
| lators. Having gained this informa-
| ton they then prepared false and
| forged affidavitS of homestead entries


upon lands within the proposed re-
| serve. In most cases these entries
purported to have been made upon
| mountainous land which was worth
| less to actual settlement but which


was quite as valuable as any other
| to the land thieves as their only pur-
pose in securing it was to exchange
| it for scrip. Scrip was worth IS


an acre so that every homestead of


100 acres for which they could Sc


cure patent had a cash value of $500


The only expense was the money ex-


pended in bribing officials.


| Petty operators in the land frauds
| were those who made these pre-
tended homestead entries and Sc


cured patents, which they Afterwards
converted into scrip. The big Grim
| inals whose trails the Government
is now pursuing were the million
aire lumber men who bought the
scrip, using it to acquire valuable
timber lands throughout the North
west. 1t was Customary also for
the big lumber men to work the
Gc,.ip
selves by colonizing with their en-
ployes or others hired for the pur-
pose, wholesale quantities of land


Immense amounts of timber land


have been acquired also by owners


of paper mills, lumber mills, and


other large concerns who have caused


claims to be taken up in the names


of their employees and then convey


ed to themselves.


Senator Mitchell's indictment is


on the specific charge that he ac-
cepted a bride of $4,000 from S. A.
D. Puter to use his influence in ex-
pediating the issuance of patents up
on certain fraudulent homestead
claims. The government claims it
has documentary evidence as well
as the confession of Puter to prove
that his payment was actually made.
But Francis J. Heney, the Govern
ment prosecutor, declares that even
if proof of this payment were lack


ing, there would still be ample evil
dence to convict him of completely in
the land frauds.


1t is intimated that the investi-
cation of Congressman Hermanns
indictment returned December 81


He may be asked to explain the dis
appearance of 3j letter books from
his office when he was Land Com
missioner. He frequently asserted
! that these books contained only pri-
vate correspondence which had


nothing to do with the Land Depart
ment, but the Government is in
possession of evidence showing that


many of the letters contained in
these books related TO land entries
in Oregon, and many Of them were ad-
dressed to persons who are now I'M
der indictment for completely in the
| land frauds.


Finest buffet in Spokane at the
| Pfister.
